config.text:
Tel Aviv Tree Planting Targets Calculator

Analysis and mapping by Evgeniya Bobkova (evgeniyab@technion.ac.il) and Or Aleksandrowicz (oraleks@technion.ac.il);

Raw datasets: the Survey of Israel, Tel Aviv municipality, Open Street Maps.

This platform allows you to select strategic locations for new street tree planting. 


First, using the filter icons at the bottom, you are asked to select which street segments need to receive additional planting, based on a variety of spatial and morphological criteria.


Then, using the left-hand sidebar, you are asked to define a calculation method to generate the total number of trees that need to be planted along these streets.


Inspect the following layers to decide which segments need planting:


Spring/Summer Shade Index: The proportion of solar radiation intercepted before reaching ground level during a typical spring/summer day, calculated per street segment (0 to 1, where 0 means full exposure and 1 means full shade). A Shade Index below 0.4 indicates insufficient shading (Aleksandrowicz et al., 2020).


Existing Trees: Trunk locations and their respective crown sizes are extracted from aerial imagery (Sharabi, 2025). Trees with a crown diameter smaller than 5 m are defined as "underdeveloped trees”, while larger crowns are counted as “shade trees”. When calculating planting targets, existing shade trees are subtracted from the total “ideal” number of required trees determined by the selected method.


Pedestrian hotspots (street centrality metrics)


Local centers: captured via closeness centrality (1 km radius), which measures how central and accessible a street is within the street network.


Transit streets: captured via betweenness

centrality (2 km and 5 km radii), identifying streets that serve as essential mediators for neighbourhood and city-wide movement. Both closeness and betweenness centrality have been proven to correlate with pedestrian flows in cities (e.g. Berghauser Pont et al., 2019; Omer and Kaplan, 2019).


Building density: measured as the Accessible Floor Space Index (FSI) within a 500 m walking distance. It is calculated as the total accessible Gross Floor Area of buildings divided by the total reachable area.


Shops and Restaurants: Total count within a 500 m walking distance from each street as an additional indicator of a vibrant street life.


Proximity analyses: Walking distances to the nearest schools, preschools, bus stops, and mass transit stations (tram/metro/rail).


Planting targets calculation methods:


Method 1 (canopy-cover-based target): Determines the number of trees required to reach a specific target tree canopy cover ratio (TCCR) for a segment, based on the segment's total area, tree crown diameter, and the expected canopy area of new trees. You can follow one global target as set by Peeters et al. (2020), set your own target, or use target TCCRs based on the actual performance of the urban forest in your city (Sharabi, 2025).


Method 2 (tree spacing): Determines tree counts based on a set physical distance between tree trunks. This scenario also calculates the resulting TCCR as a secondary output. Results are provided as both "Number of new trees to plant" and "Total number of trees” (ignoring existing trees).


References

Aleksandrowicz, O., Zur, S., Lebendiger, Y., Lerman, Y., 2020. Shade maps for prioritizing municipal microclimatic action in hot climates: Learning from Tel Aviv-Yafo. Sustainable Cities and Society 53, 101931. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scs.2019.101931

Berghauser Pont, M., Stavroulaki, G., Marcus, L., 2019. Development of urban types based on network centrality, built density and their impact on pedestrian movement. Environment and Planning B: Urban Analytics and City Science 46, 1549–1564. https://doi.org/10.1177/2399808319852632

Omer, I., Kaplan, N., 2019. Structural properties of the angular and metric street network’s centralities and their implications for movement flows. Environment and Planning B: Urban Analytics and City Science 46, 1182–1200. https://doi.org/10.1177/2399808318760571

Peeters, A., Shashua-Bar, L., Meir, S., Shmulevich, R.R., Caspi, Y., Weyl, M., Motzafi-Haller, W., Angel, N., 2020. A decision support tool for calculating effective shading in urban streets. Urban Climate 34, 100672. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.uclim.2020.100672

Sharabi, S., 2025. Quantifying urban tree planting targets based on data from available sources (Master Thesis). Technion Israel Institute of Technology, Haifa.
